danger_signals.meaning: player character taking damage; vulnerability increasing
critical danger; potential knockout loss imminent

safety_signals.visual_form: increased screen space between boxers in side-scrolling view
safety_signals.meaning: reduced immediate threat; window for recovery or setup

safety_signals.visual_form: opponent animation pauses or reduced movement speed post-exchange
advancement through fictional fighter roster (Dynamite Joe, Fernando Gomez, Kim Nang, King Jason, Ironhead)
safety_signals.meaning: survival of bout; progression toward championship goal

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: side-scrolling screen with two full-screen-sized boxers in combat arena

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: camera shifts to observe boxers compete without player input
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: AI-controlled boxers execute autonomous fight sequences; demonstrates character movesets

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: inclusion of James Buster Douglas as playable/opponent fighter in Genesis port (1990 release, immediately following Tyson upset)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: character available in championship run context; represents real-world sporting narrative integration

environmental_storytelling.example: Dynamite Joe (The Miracle Fighter), Fernando Gomez (The South American Eagle), Kim Nang (The Korean Comet) — names and titles communicate regional origin and fighting reputation without explicit world-building dialogue
Single-season championship run structure implies tournament bracket narrative; facing successive opponents tells story of climbing ranks toward title without cutscenes

environmental_storytelling.visual_form: James Buster Douglas inclusion as Genesis exclusive character immediately post-Tyson victory (1990)
environmental_storytelling.example: game leverages contemporary sports narrative (Tyson upset) to communicate world-state relevance; real athlete presence signals topical political/cultural moment
Buster Douglas uses The Detroit Kid's base graphics with palette swap — visual economy implies character genealogy; both share Detroit association, suggesting regional identity persistence

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: left-right movement control available; punch execution timing-dependent; position relative to opponent determines strike success
initiates punches; advances screen space; applies knockback pressure on player
reduced animation speed; limited offensive capability; window opens for player final blow execution
falls to canvas; visual count-down implied; potential knockout outcome if unable to recover
autonomous execution of full fight routines without player input; demonstrates moveset variety and fighting style differentiation

notes: James 'Buster' Douglas Knockout Boxing is a side-scrolling boxing arcade port (Taito Final Blow, 1988) renamed and rebranded for Sega Genesis (1990) to capitalize on Douglas's real-world upset victory over Mike Tyson. Manifestation patterns center on timing-based combat feedback, opponent state visualization through animation, and roster-based tournament progression. World-state communication relies primarily on opponent selection/difficulty escalation and spectator mode affordance rather than explicit environmental or dialogue systems. Faction identity is conveyed through fighter nomenclature, regional epithets, and visual model differentiation. Political significance emerges through Douglas character inclusion as topical real-athlete branding rather than narrative-driven worldbuilding.
